      "description": "Establish the absolute bedrock of system design by understanding the physical constraints of hardware, memory hierarchy, and latency.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Recite and apply the 'Latencies Every Programmer Should Know' to identify the exact physical bottleneck (CPU, Memory, Disk, Network) in any given system architecture.",
        "concepts": [
          "Memory Hierarchy",
          "L1/L2 Cache Latencies vs. Main Memory",
          "Disk Seek Times vs. SSD operations",

      "title": "I/O Models & High-Performance Concurrency",

      "description": "Deep dive into how operating systems handle thousands of concurrent connections without collapsing, examining kernel-level I/O mechanics.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Explain the difference between OS-level threads and user-space coroutines, and trace how epoll and io_uring enable non-blocking asynchronous I/O.",

      "plan": {

      "plan": {

        "concepts": [
          "Disk Pages vs. Blocks",
          "Random vs. Sequential Disk Writes",
          "B-Trees (Read-optimized, in-place updates)",

          "MemTables, SSTables, and Compaction",
          "Write-Ahead Logs (WAL)"

          "Phase 3: Data Architecture (The Memory)",

parsed.goal: Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.

modules.title: Hardware Primitives & The Physics of Latency
modules.description: Establish the absolute bedrock of system design by understanding the physical constraints of hardware, memory hierarchy, and latency.

plan.goal: Recite and apply the 'Latencies Every Programmer Should Know' to identify the exact physical bottleneck (CPU, Memory, Disk, Network) in any given system architecture.
concepts: L1/L2 Cache Latencies vs. Main Memory
Disk Seek Times vs. SSD operations
Network physical limits (speed of light constraints)
sourceRefs: Phase 1: The Hardware & OS Layer (The Physics)
Layer 1: The Foundations (The 'Physics' of System Design)

modules.title: I/O Models & High-Performance Concurrency
modules.description: Deep dive into how operating systems handle thousands of concurrent connections without collapsing, examining kernel-level I/O mechanics.

plan.goal: Explain the difference between OS-level threads and user-space coroutines, and trace how epoll and io_uring enable non-blocking asynchronous I/O.
concepts: Process vs. Thread vs. Coroutine (Goroutines/Green threads)
sourceRefs: Phase 1: The Hardware & OS Layer (The Physics)
Layer 1: The Foundations (The 'Physics' of System Design)
prerequisites: Hardware Primitives & The Physics of Latency

modules.title: Transport Layer Deep Dive: TCP, UDP, & QUIC
modules.description: Move beyond the application layer to understand how packets actually traverse networks and why transport protocols dictate system latency.

plan.goal: Map out the TCP 3-way handshake, analyze the impact of TCP Slow Start on latency, and justify the transition to UDP-based QUIC for modern protocols.
concepts: TCP Congestion Control (Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance)
Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking in HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2
prerequisites: Hardware Primitives & The Physics of Latency

modules.title: RPC, Serialization, & Schema Evolution
modules.description: Analyze how machines communicate structured data across the wire, focusing on binary serialization efficiency and the multiplexing power of gRPC.

plan.goal: Compare the binary parsing mechanics of Protocol Buffers against JSON, and architect a schema evolution strategy for a distributed microservice environment.
concepts: String parsing overhead vs. binary decoding
prerequisites: Transport Layer Deep Dive: TCP, UDP, & QUIC

modules.title: Storage Engine Mechanics: B-Trees vs. LSM-Trees
modules.description: Deconstruct databases down to how bytes are physically written to disk, contrasting read-optimized structures with write-optimized log structures.

plan.goal: Diagram the life cycle of a write operation in both a B-Tree database and an LSM-Tree database, justifying which to use based on read/write ratios.
sourceRefs: Phase 1: The Hardware & OS Layer (The Physics)

modules.title: Database Indexing Anatomy
modules.description: Study the internal mechanics of database indexes, understanding how they accelerate retrieval and the overhead they introduce.

plan.goal: Design a composite indexing strategy for a complex query workload, demonstrating how covered queries prevent disk page lookups.
prerequisites: Storage Engine Mechanics: B-Trees vs. LSM-Trees

modules.title: Transaction Theory, Isolation Levels, & MVCC
modules.description: Examine exactly how databases handle concurrent operations without corrupting data, moving far beyond the basic acronym of ACID.

plan.goal: Map specific read phenomena (Dirty, Phantom, Non-repeatable) to their corresponding ANSI SQL isolation levels, and explain how MVCC resolves them without table locks.
concepts: Isolation Levels (Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable)
Concurrency Phenomena (Dirty reads, Phantom reads, Lost updates)

modules.title: Distributed Laws: CAP, PACELC & Quorums
modules.description: Confront the physical and logical realities of networking multiple machines together, framing the hard trade-offs that system architects must make.

plan.goal: Mathematically justify read/write quorum configurations (R + W > N) and apply the PACELC theorem to choose between latency and consistency during network health.
concepts: Tuning read/write quorums for speed vs. integrity
sourceRefs: Phase 4: Distributed Systems Theory (The Laws of Physics)

modules.title: Time, Ordering, and Causality in Distributed Systems
modules.description: Unlearn reliance on system clocks. Discover how distributed systems establish causality and order events when global time is a physical impossibility.

plan.goal: Trace the sequence of distributed events using Lamport Clocks and Vector Clocks to identify causality and resolve conflicts.
concepts: The Myth of Wall Clock Time
sourceRefs: Phase 4: Distributed Systems Theory (The Laws of Physics)

modules.title: Consensus & Coordination Algorithms
modules.description: Understand exactly how a cluster of independent nodes agrees on a single source of truth, exploring the mechanics of Raft and Paxos.

plan.goal: Model a Leader Election and Log Replication sequence using the Raft consensus algorithm, identifying failure states and recovery paths.
sourceRefs: Phase 4: Distributed Systems Theory (The Laws of Physics)
prerequisites: Time, Ordering, and Causality in Distributed Systems

modules.title: Load Balancing & Consistent Hashing
modules.description: Examine the mathematical and networking principles used to distribute millions of requests across clusters without creating hot spots or bottlenecks.

plan.goal: Differentiate Layer 4 vs. Layer 7 load balancing, and mathematically simulate how Consistent Hashing prevents a cache stampede during node failure.
prerequisites: Transport Layer Deep Dive: TCP, UDP, & QUIC

modules.title: Caching Mechanics & State Management
modules.description: Move beyond 'just use Redis.' Understand cache topologies, consistency challenges, and the algorithms that determine what data stays in memory.

plan.goal: Design a caching topology that prevents the Thundering Herd problem while maintaining acceptable eventual consistency between cache and primary storage.
concepts: Cache Aside vs. Write-Through vs. Write-Behind (Write-back)
Cache Eviction Policies (LRU, LFU, FIFO, ARC)
The Thundering Herd / Cache Stampede problem
prerequisites: Storage Engine Mechanics: B-Trees vs. LSM-Trees

modules.title: Database Sharding & Data Partitioning
modules.description: Learn how to physically split databases across multiple machines, exploring the deep complexities of distributed queries and hot keys.

plan.goal: Select an optimal Shard Key for a high-throughput data model to ensure uniform distribution and prevent 'Hot Key' node exhaustion.
concepts: Global Secondary Indexes in sharded environments

modules.title: Asynchronous Processing & Delivery Guarantees
modules.description: Master message queues, event brokers, and the intricate realities of exactly-once vs. at-least-once message delivery in distributed networks.

plan.goal: Architect a distributed worker pipeline using message brokers that safely handles retries under 'At-least-once' delivery without corrupting state through idempotency.
concepts: At-most-once, At-least-once, and Exactly-once delivery semantics

modules.title: Fault Tolerance, Backpressure & Rate Limiting
modules.description: Equip systems to survive failure. Explore the algorithms that protect infrastructure from being overwhelmed by traffic spikes or cascading outages.

plan.goal: Implement rate limiting using Token Bucket algorithms, and design a Circuit Breaker pattern with exponential backoff and jitter to prevent cascading failures.
concepts: Retries with Exponential Backoff and Jitter
Rate Limiting Algorithms: Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket
Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window counters

modules.title: Advanced Data Patterns: Event Sourcing & CQRS
modules.description: Radically shift how state is viewed: from mutating records in place to storing immutable logs of historical events.

plan.goal: Design a system utilizing CQRS and Event Sourcing, explaining how to replay state, manage read models, and ensure perfect auditability.
concepts: Replaying time and handling schema changes in events

modules.title: The Internals of Observability
modules.description: Design the telemetry backbone required to debug highly distributed, concurrent systems without resorting to SSHing into individual machines.

plan.goal: Architect an observability pipeline combining time-series metrics, structured logging, and distributed request tracing to debug a microservice latency spike.
concepts: The Three Pillars: Metrics, Logging, Tracing
Distributed Tracing mechanics (Jaeger/Zipkin, Trace IDs, Spans)

modules.title: Capstone: Engineering a Global-Scale Platform
modules.description: Synthesize all layers from hardware primitives to distributed consensus by reverse-engineering and designing a massive-scale system from absolute scratch.

plan.goal: Produce a bottom-up architectural design for a Netflix-scale video streaming service, mathematically justifying storage engines, consensus protocols, and transport layer decisions.
concepts: System Ingestion and Chunking via Distributed Workers
Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) and Edge routing
Applying Circuit Breakers and Fallbacks in delivery
Cell-Based Architecture for blast radius containment
sourceRefs: Worked Example: Designing a Global Video Streaming Service (Netflix-scale)

modules.title: Hardware Primitives & The Physics of Latency
Transport Layer Deep Dive: TCP, UDP, & QUIC
Storage Engine Mechanics: B-Trees vs. LSM-Trees
Time, Ordering, and Causality in Distributed Systems
Advanced Data Patterns: Event Sourcing & CQRS
